summary:
Sejong the Great (r. 1418–1450 AD) was the fourth king of the Joseon dynasty and the most celebrated ruler in Korean history — a scholar-king who personally oversaw or participated in advances in science, agriculture, music, and linguistics that transformed Korean civilization, most notably through his sponsorship of the creation of Hangul, the Korean alphabet, in 1443. Korea had previously relied on Chinese characters (Hanja) for writing — a system that required years of study and effectively restricted literacy to the educated elite. Hangul, a phonetic alphabet of 28 letters (later reduced to 24) designed to represent the sounds of spoken Korean, could be learned in days rather than years, and Sejong explicitly designed it to be accessible to ordinary people: his introduction stated, 'A wise man can acquaint himself with them before the morning is over; a stupid man can learn them in the space of ten days.'

Sejong established the Hall of Worthies (Jiphyeonjeon), a royal research institute staffed by the kingdom's most talented scholars, which produced advances in astronomy (new instruments and celestial maps), agriculture (the Nongsa jikseol, a practical farming manual based on Korean conditions rather than Chinese models), medicine (the Hyang yak jipseong bang, a comprehensive Korean pharmacopoeia), and music notation. His scientific innovations included rain gauges (cheugugi), water clocks, and sundials. Despite opposition from conservative Confucian scholars who resisted Hangul as undermining Chinese cultural dominance, the alphabet survived and gradually became universal — now recognized as one of the world's most linguistically sophisticated writing systems, uniquely associated with a single ruler's deliberate design.
